bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 26
bibliographic.title: Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse

entities.description: The U-M source issue. Its title page says re-collated with the manuscript, enlarged and revised 1913, while publication is dated 1867, 1914 and the series statement says 1914 (for 1913). Furnivall collated through p. 18 in 1906; nine pieces were added; an unidentified collaborator freshly collated the whole; C. W. Foster checked readings; and O. T. Williams supplied a new glossary.
entities.label: Perry, Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse, revised 1913 and issued 1914

rights.basis: The revised edition was published in 1914, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff described in U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A.

access.accessMethod: Advance written application, appointment, and academic letter under current readers guidance; weekday research hours are 10:00-12:30 and 14:00-16:00.

localCopies.label: Lincoln Cathedral readers and researchers guidance

access.accessMethod: Request digital images or photocopies for exact target folios, or enquire about a complete-codex research reproduction; fees and permission apply.
access.entityLabel: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91
access.format: Digital images or photocopies by request
notes: Ask for ff. 197r-209v, 213v-218v, and 271r-276r; for codicological context, request the complete manuscript rather than cropped target leaves alone.

access.accessMethod: No public complete manuscript viewer, IIIF Presentation manifest, download-all route, or target-folio image sequence was verified; only the f. 80r partial image is exposed.
access.entityLabel: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91

entities.description: Robert Thornton's c. 1430-1450 paper miscellany and the sole base witness for all three Perry/U-M selected works: Mirror item 38 at ff. 197r-209v, Gaytryge item 47 at ff. 213v-218v, and Abbey item 59 at ff. 271r-276r.

historical_designations: shelfmark B recorded by Thomas Tanner in 1700
holding.notes: At Lincoln by 1700; current official record identifies Robert Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire, as sole scribe.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91

physicalDescription.extent: 322 leaves surviving from an original minimum of 340
physicalDescription.format: Large religious, didactic, medical, and romance miscellany
notes: Nineteenth-century foliation 1-133, 133A, 134-321.
The selected items are non-contiguous and occur in manuscript order Mirror, Gaytryge, Abbey, unlike Perry's printed order.

rights.basis: The texts and manuscript copying are medieval; this does not apply to photographs, catalog prose, or access contracts.
rights.component: Medieval textual content

entities.description: The printed Latin source from which Perry reproduces a parallel passage beside Arundel 288 and the Thornton Mirror. Modern research identifies multiple Latin states and retranslations, so this printed parallel is not asserted as Thornton's direct exemplar.

rights.basis: The historical printed source and Latin text predate modern copyright.

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

entities.description: Late-fourteenth-century large religious miscellany containing a Mirror version at ff. 356r-359v and an Abbey version at ff. 359v-361r in the current digital foliation. Perry explicitly prints the Abbey in a four-witness conspectus; it is collateral, not his base.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves; originally viii + 413
notes: The Mirror ends and Abbey begins together on f. 359v, IIIF canvas 605.

rights.basis: The late-fourteenth-century texts and copying predate modern copyright; image rights are separate.
rights.component: Medieval Mirror and Abbey texts

access.entityLabel: Pamela L. C. Greig, The 'Lay Folks' Catechism'—An Edition (2018)

rights.basis: The repository PDF states All Rights Reserved and supplies no open-content license.
rights.component: Thesis and modern critical edition

bibliographic.citation: Pamela L. C. Greig, The 'Lay Folks' Catechism'—An Edition (PhD thesis, University of Nottingham, 2018).
bibliographic.title: The 'Lay Folks' Catechism'—An Edition

entities.description: Modern critical edition using Bodleian Don.c.13 as base and recording twenty-six Gaytryge witnesses, including complete, reworked, and fragmentary copies. It is a collateral census, not an intermediary in Perry's source chain.
entities.label: Pamela L. C. Greig, The 'Lay Folks' Catechism'—An Edition (2018)
notes: Complete: A BL Add 25006 ff. 1r-9v; C Oxford Corpus Christi 155 ff. 239r-250v; G Paris Bibliothèque Sainte-Geneviève 3390 ff. 37v-52v; H BL Harley 1022 ff. 66r-73v, acephalous from line 83; L Lincoln MS 91 ff. 213v-218v; N Nottingham WLC/LM/9 ff. 248r-256r; S Sidney Sussex 55 ff. 41r-47v; T Borthwick Abp Reg 11 ff. 295r-297r; V Trinity B.10.12 ff. 56r-67r; X BL Arundel 507 ff. 50r-54r; Z Bodleian Don.c.13 ff. 162va-165va.

Reworked: B Bodley 789 ff. 51v-68v, acephalous from line 50; E BL Harley 6615 ff. 127v-140v; La Lambeth 408 ff. 1r-17r; R Rawlinson C.288 ff. 85r-91r; U one former codex now Bodleian Eng.th.e.181 ff. 6r-10v plus Douce 274 ff. 1r-17v; W Keio MS 9, formerly Hopton Hall, ff. 3v-9v; Y Beinecke 317 ff. 21v-22r and 30v-34r.

Fragments: D CUL Dd.12.39 ff. 1v-2v; F CUL Ff.5.40 ff. 117v-119r; J CUL Ii.4.9 ff. 95v-96r; K CUL Add 6686, formerly Ashburnham 140, pp. 362-364; M BL Add 24202 ff. 35v-36v; O Chetham 60 bifolium, modern pp. 3-4; P Rawlinson C.285 ff. 61v-62r; Q Queen's College Oxford 389B fragments 24-25, formerly ff. 245 and 248 of MS 389.
Excluded false witnesses: Lincoln MS 66 (old siglum I), Trinity B.14.19 (old O), and York Minster XVI.L.12. Douce 273 is physically associated with the split U codex but carries no target text.

rights.basis: The repository PDF states All Rights Reserved and supplies no open-content license.
rights.component: Thesis and modern critical edition

rights.basis: The cached author manuscript states copyright in the author and CC BY-NC-ND 3.0.
rights.component: Author manuscript

bibliographic.citation: Ian Cornelius, 'The Lay Folks' Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus', Review of English Studies 70 (2019): 14-36, doi:10.1093/res/hgy107.
bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks' Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus

entities.description: Peer-reviewed study of the 1357 commission, twenty-six-witness tradition, and form. It treats Abp Reg 11 and Don.c.13 as especially authoritative and argues that the English instruction is not securely classifiable as traditional alliterative verse.
entities.label: Ian Cornelius, 'The Lay Folks' Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus' (2019)

rights.basis: The cached author manuscript states copyright in the author and CC BY-NC-ND 3.0.
rights.component: Author manuscript

localCopies.label: Cambridge Core public Watson abstract page

bibliographic.citation: Nicholas Watson, 'Middle English Versions and Audiences of Edmund of Abingdon's Speculum Religiosorum', in Texts and Traditions of Medieval Pastoral Care (2009), doi:10.1017/9781846157691.011.
bibliographic.title: Middle English Versions and Audiences of Edmund of Abingdon's Speculum Religiosorum

entities.description: Current synthesis distinguishing multiple late-fourteenth-century Middle English translations. It reports about eight translations, thirteen manuscripts, and two early printed editions, and treats the three complete prose translations in Vernon, Simeon, and Thornton as independent.
entities.label: Nicholas Watson, Middle English versions and audiences of Edmund's Speculum Religiosorum (2009)

rights.basis: Cambridge Core restricts full chapter access and displays no open license for the chapter.
rights.component: Modern scholarly chapter and abstract page

access.entityLabel: Clare Goymer, parallel-text Mirror thesis (1961)

rights.basis: The repository says copyright Clare Rosemary Goymer and acknowledges permission to archive for study and educational use; it does not grant a general open-content license.
rights.component: Thesis accepted version

bibliographic.citation: Clare Rosemary Goymer, A Parallel Text Edition of the Middle English Prose Version(s) of the 'Mirror of St. Edmund' Based on the Known Complete Manuscripts (MA thesis, Royal Holloway, 1961).
bibliographic.title: A Parallel Text Edition of the Middle English Prose Version(s) of the 'Mirror of St. Edmund' Based on the Known Complete Manuscripts

entities.description: Parallel-text study of ten Middle English prose versions: three translated from Anglo-Norman, four from Latin, and three of mixed affiliation. Six complete manuscripts and an early printed text are fully collated; incomplete versions and the interpolated Rolle attribution are treated in appendices.
entities.label: Clare Goymer, parallel-text Mirror thesis (1961)

rights.basis: The repository says copyright Clare Rosemary Goymer and acknowledges permission to archive for study and educational use; it does not grant a general open-content license.
rights.component: Thesis accepted version

entities.description: Current exact-title query returning twelve occurrence rows, not twelve physical manuscripts or all known versions. It is used alongside, not as a replacement for, Watson, Goymer, Thompson, and older repertory counts.
entities.label: Index of Middle English Prose: Mirror of St Edmund title results

rights.basis: The site credits compiled indexes and Boydell & Brewer database access but exposes no open-content license for wholesale reuse.

access.entityLabel: Boyda Johnstone, Theatre of the Mind (2010)

workPortion.label: Abbey witness census, Stowe images, transcription, and analysis
locators: Stowe MS 39 ff. 4r-8r images and quotations throughout

rights.basis: The Library and Archives Canada copy records a non-exclusive preservation/reproduction permission while the author retains copyright; substantial extracts or image reuse require separate review.
rights.component: Thesis, transcriptions, and embedded manuscript images

bibliographic.citation: Boyda Johnstone, Theatre of the Mind: Devotional Performance in British Library MS Stowe 39 (MA thesis, University of Calgary, 2010).
bibliographic.title: Theatre of the Mind: Devotional Performance in British Library MS Stowe 39

entities.description: Study of devotional performance in Stowe MS 39 with a detailed Abbey witness table, Stowe images and quotations, audience analysis, French-source discussion, and explicit attribution cautions.
entities.label: Boyda Johnstone, Theatre of the Mind (2010)
notes: Listed carriers: NLW Peniarth 334A pp. 77-123; CUL Ii.4.9 ff. 69v-87r, Ll.5.18 ff. 1r-24v, Dd.11.89 ff. 2r-8v; Jesus College Q.D.4(46) ff. 146r-172r; Trinity O.1.29(1053) ff. 77r-101v; Lincoln MS 91 ff. 271r-276r; BL Add 22283 ff. 165r-168r, Add 36983 ff. 281r-297v, Egerton 3245 ff. 156v-183r, Harley 1704 ff. 37v-48r, Harley 2406 ff. 61r-83r, Harley 5272 ff. 105r-137v, Stowe 39 ff. 4r-8r; Lambeth 432 ff. 37v-68r.

Further carriers: Bodleian Douce 141 ff. 139r-145r, Douce 323 ff. 140v-159v, Eng. poet. a.1 ff. 359v-361r in current digital foliation, Laud misc. 210 ff. 136r-146v and 180r-186v (two occurrences); Oxford Corpus Christi 155 ff. 260r-268v, completed by BL Cotton Vespasian D.XIII f. 181r; Stonyhurst B.xxiii ff. 45v-60r; Keio MS 9, formerly Hopton Hall, pp. 27-43; private Bradford-Lawrence 8 ff. 1r-6v; Winchester College 33 ff. 94r-108v.

Johnstone states twenty-four manuscripts but lists twenty-five carriers; Stowe was unknown to Consacro, and the Cotton leaf is an end fragment physically supplementing the Corpus 155 occurrence rather than an independent complete copy.
Three Wynkyn de Worde editions are named: two in 1496 and one in 1500. Eighteen manuscript copies are accompanied by the Charter.

rights.basis: The Library and Archives Canada copy records a non-exclusive preservation/reproduction permission while the author retains copyright; substantial extracts or image reuse require separate review.
rights.component: Thesis, transcriptions, and embedded manuscript images

entities.description: Full study of Thornton's compiling and source contexts, with substantial discussion and quotation of all three target works. It reports at least twenty Mirror manuscripts and twenty-four Abbey manuscripts, while its older five-print Abbey count is retained as bibliographic history rather than silently reconciled.

next_steps: Obtain and review the complete Goymer thesis and Watson chapter.
Compare Thornton against all version groups rather than assuming Perry's Arundel and Magna Bibliotheca parallels are exemplars.
Reconcile complete manuscripts, physical carriers, occurrence rows, extracts, and fragments before stating a normalized count.

next_steps: Consult Consacro's critical edition and Buhler's incunable study.
Separate the Cotton end fragment from the Corpus 155 occurrence, duplicate Laud occurrences, Stowe's post-Consacro discovery, and physical carrier count.
Verify bibliographic editions versus surviving print copies.
openQuestions.question: Why does Johnstone state twenty-four Abbey manuscripts while listing twenty-five carriers, and why does Thompson report five early prints while Johnstone names three editions?

next_steps: Consult EETS production correspondence and council minutes.
Retain Furnivall, Foster, and Williams's explicit roles without expanding J. M. or assigning the anonymous collation.

relations.subjectLabel: John J. Thompson, Robert Thornton and Book-Producing in the Later Middle Ages (1983)

reviewNotes: The cataloged unit is a three-work editorial selection. Robert Thornton is a scribe and compiler, not the author named by the inherited XML AUTHOR element.
The 1913 date belongs to re-collation, enlargement, and revision work; the source issue and its title page are dated 1914 and the EETS series statement says 1914 (for 1913).
The 1889 scan retains the 1867 title-page setting and has an 1889-dated publisher backlist. It is modeled as an issue/reprint version of 1867, not a separate textual edition.

Gaytryge's modern twenty-six-witness census is preserved in the Greig entity as complete, reworked, and fragmentary carriers with historical shelfmarks and rejected false witnesses. Only Lincoln MS 91 is Perry's base; Trinity B.10.12 is an explicit comparator.

Mirror counts describe different units: Watson reports thirteen manuscripts and two early printed editions for the broad Middle English prose tradition; Goymer distinguishes ten translation versions and fully collates six complete manuscripts plus an early print; Thompson says vernacular translations and adaptations survive in at least twenty manuscripts; the current IMEP exact-title query returns twelve occurrence rows, while Wells's repertory count of twenty-three includes complete texts, extracts, and fragments. These are not silently normalized into one witness total.

The researched Mirror union includes Thornton; Vernon; Simeon/Add MS 22283; Bodley 416; Douce 25; e Mus. 232; Add MS 10053; Harley 2398 (two partial translations); Harley 4012 (fragment); CUL Ii.6.40 (interpolated); CUL Ii.6.43 (extracts/fragments); CUL Ff.2.38 (extracts); Lambeth 3597; Queen's College Oxford 324; Peniarth 395; Trinity B.14.50, O.1.74, and R.3.21; HM 502; and the 1521 Copeland/Wynkyn de Worde print. Creed extracts in Add MS 60577 and Edinburgh University MS 93, and a similar HM 501 passage, remain derivative or candidate occurrences rather than secure complete witnesses.

Johnstone says the Abbey survives in twenty-four manuscripts but tabulates twenty-five physical carriers when Stowe 39 and the Cotton end fragment attached to the Corpus 155 text are retained. That discrepancy and the difference between three named Wynkyn de Worde editions and Thompson's older five-print count remain explicit.

Complete IIIF cache means every image exposed by the provider's public object at the chosen access size, not preservation-master completeness. The Vernon and Borthwick bundles passed ZIP and inventory integrity checks.

Every cached object records target-work presence semantically: present always has an exact work_portion; absent never has one. Metadata and catalog files that merely describe a work are absent under this physical/textual test.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies University of Michigan HTI item ThrnReligP, a 1997 OCR-derived encoding of only the first three prose works in the 1914 issue of George G. Perry's Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse. The issue is an enlarged and revised version whose work was completed in 1913 but whose title page is dated 1914 (for 1913); it descends from Perry's 1867 edition, while an 1889 object is an issue or reprint of the 1867 setting rather than a newly edited text. Perry transcribed all three selected works from Robert Thornton's Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 and explicitly compared only Trinity B.10.12 for Gaytryge, Arundel 288 and a printed Latin text for the Mirror, and the Vernon, Lambeth 432, and Trinity O.1.29 copies for the Abbey. Modern witness censuses are collateral: they do not retroactively become sources of Perry's or U-M's readings.

catalogSummary.author: Part-specific known and anonymous authors
catalogSummary.date: 1357 (Gaytryge); late fourteenth to early fifteenth century (Thornton Mirror version); fourteenth century (Abbey translation); copied by Thornton c. 1430-1450; electronic selection 1997
catalogSummary.editor: George Gresley Perry (1867); re-collated, enlarged, and revised in 1913 by Furnivall and other collaborators; U-M HTI electronic selection (1997)
catalogSummary.region: England, with York-archdiocese and northern-English associations for Gaytryge and the Thornton copies
catalogSummary.title: Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse (three-prose-piece selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse (three-prose-piece selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual/content unit delimited by the pinned U-M XML: the first three prose works in the 1914 issue of George G. Perry's EETS volume, namely Dan Jon Gaytryge's Sermon, The Mirror of Saint Edmund, and The Abbey of the Holy Ghost. This is a three-work editorial selection, not one medieval work, not the complete Perry volume, and not a Robert Thornton-authored collection. Robert Thornton copied the selected manuscript versions; work-level responsibility, dates, genres, and witness traditions remain part-specific.

contentParts.label: Dan Jon Gaytryge's Sermon
notes: Fourteen P and four NOTE1 elements; the other twenty-five known modern-census witnesses, including complete, reworked, and fragmentary copies, are collateral to Perry's Thornton source.
contentParts.summary: A six-part vernacular pastoral instruction explaining the articles of faith, commandments, sacraments, bodily works of mercy, virtues, and deadly sins for parish teaching and confession.

contentParts.label: The Fourteen Articles of the Creed
contentParts.summary: Explains seven articles concerning the Godhead and seven concerning Christ's humanity.

contentParts.label: The Ten Commandments
contentParts.summary: Explains duties toward God and neighbor under the ten commandments.

contentParts.label: The Seven Sacraments of Holy Church
contentParts.summary: Defines the seven sacraments and their proper reception.

contentParts.label: The Seven Bodily Works of Mercy
contentParts.summary: Lists works of mercy toward people in bodily need.

contentParts.label: The Seven Spiritual Virtues
contentParts.summary: Describes virtues that order Christian conduct and endurance.

contentParts.label: The Seven Deadly Sins
contentParts.summary: Defines and warns against pride, envy, wrath, sloth, covetousness, gluttony, and lust.

contentParts.label: The Mirror of Saint Edmund
notes: Twenty-nine P and three NOTE1 elements. Perry's Latin-style chapter numbers are absent because they are editorial and do not occur in the Thornton witness.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English devotional translation in the broad Speculum Religiosorum tradition, teaching prayer, custody of the senses, virtues, self-examination, Christ's passion, and contemplation of God.

contentParts.label: The Abbey of the Holy Ghost
notes: Seven P and three NOTE1 elements. The selected work is The Abbey alone; the companion Charter of the Abbey of the Holy Ghost is outside the XML boundary.
contentParts.summary: A devotional allegory for people unable to enter bodily religion, organizing an interior abbey through conscience, virtues, disciplined senses, prayer, charity, and contemplative desire.

dateStatements.display: 1357, in connection with Archbishop John Thoresby's pastoral instruction
Late fourteenth century at earliest; the exact Thornton version predates its c. 1430-1450 copy
notes: This range does not force all Middle English Mirror versions into one translation event.
The witness date is not a shared composition date for the three works.

scope.description: Attestation of the exact Thornton copies represented by Perry and U-M

dateStatements.display: Re-collated, enlarged, and revised 1913; issued with a 1914 title-page date (for 1913)

scope.description: Revision work on the Perry edition, distinct from the 1914 issue/publication date

notes: This dates the U-M selection, not the medieval works.

extent.description: Three top-level prose works, with six nested catechetical sections in Dan Jon Gaytryge's Sermon. The electronic text spans Perry's printed pp. 1-62 and Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 ff. 197r-218v and 271r-276r in non-contiguous manuscript order.

assertion.notes: Perry and Skeat proposed alliterative verse behind Gaytryge, and some witnesses lineate or point the text; modern scholarship cautions that it is not securely traditional alliterative verse. The selected Thornton/Perry/U-M presentation remains prose.

formStatements.description: All three selected parts are encoded and printed as prose.

scope.description: All three separately modeled works in the selected unit

genres.label: Edited selection
notes: This whole-scope term describes the heterogeneous electronic unit, not a medieval genre.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin headings, citations, and prayers occur as embedded material; Perry suppresses two Latin prayers in the Mirror where he prints their complete English equivalents.
Witness dates, holdings, shelfmarks, digital access, and image rights are modeled in the lineage manifest and do not become whole-selection descriptive properties.

The part-level witness censuses are collateral research layers. Perry's selected readings and U-M's text trace only through Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 except where Perry explicitly prints or discusses comparisons.
The date phrase 're-collated with the MS., enlarged and revised 1913' describes revision work; the physical source issue is dated 1914 and also styled 1914 (for 1913).
Content-part item counts are non-overlapping: the three work parents each count one item, while the six nested Gaytryge sections count zero additional works.

next_steps: Consult the complete Goymer thesis and Watson chapter where access permits.
Collate the Thornton version against the full Latin, Anglo-Norman, and other Middle English version groups.
Keep Perry's Arundel 288 and Magna Bibliotheca parallels as comparison sources, not historical exemplars.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin or Anglo-Norman state directly underlies the Thornton Middle English Mirror, and how should the differing counts of complete versions, manuscripts, occurrences, extracts, and fragments be reconciled?

next_steps: Consult Consacro's complete dissertation and Buhler's incunable bibliography.
Distinguish physical carriers, duplicate occurrences, attached end fragments, and bibliographic editions before normalizing a count.
openQuestions.question: How should the Abbey tradition's stated twenty-four-manuscript count be reconciled with Johnstone's twenty-five listed carriers, and the three Wynkyn de Worde editions with Thompson's older five-print count?

next_steps: Consult EETS correspondence, council minutes, and production archives for OS 26 and the 1913 reprint.
Do not expand initials or assign the anonymous collation without documentary evidence.
openQuestions.question: Who performed the fresh whole-volume collation reported in the 1913 revision note, and who was the note's signatory J. M.?

next_steps: Seek legacy HTI project documentation if the rationale becomes catalogically important; retain the explicit XML boundary without speculation.
openQuestions.question: Why did U-M select exactly Perry's first three prose works?

scope.description: Broad origin of the three Middle English works; not a single place of composition

notes: A witness association is not automatically a place of composition or translation.
This responsibility is limited to the first work and does not extend to the edited selection.

subjects.label: Interior cloister, contemplative practice, and lay devotion

summaries.value: This three-work prose selection joins John de Gaytryge's practical instruction on the creed, commandments, sacraments, works of mercy, virtues, and deadly sins; a Middle English version of Edmund of Abingdon's Mirror, guiding prayer, self-knowledge, virtue, and contemplation; and The Abbey of the Holy Ghost, an allegory that invites people unable to enter bodily religion to construct a disciplined interior cloister. The works share pastoral and devotional concerns but have separate authorship, translation histories, dates, and witness traditions.

The record stops at the three DIV1 units in ThrnReligP.xml and preserves the six nested Gaytryge divisions. It excludes Perry's preface, table of contents, marginal glosses, glossary, index, and sixteen later numbered works; those excluded materials remain relevant only as edition and editorial-practice evidence.

Robert Thornton is the scribe and compiler of the selected Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 copies, not the author of the three works. The XML source description inherits an AUTHOR element naming Thornton from the bibliographic title, while its individual incipits and modern scholarship establish distinct part-level responsibilities.

The pinned XML has three DIV1 work units, six DIV2 sections nested in Gaytryge, nineteen HEAD, fifty-four P, ten NOTE1, forty-eight manuscript-leaf MILESTONE, sixty-one PB, sixty-two EPB, and five CORR elements. The three works contain respectively 14, 29, and 7 P elements and 4, 3, and 3 notes.

notes: The qualifier exposes the exact electronic boundary and avoids presenting Perry's nineteen-item volume or Thornton's manuscript as one authored work.
titles.value: Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse (three-prose-piece selection)

notes: Exact electronic header title; the XML itself says that only the first three prose pieces are present.
titles.value: Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse / edited ... by George G. Perry

notes: Also cataloged in modern scholarship as The Lay Folks' Catechism; the Perry/U-M heading is retained as the part label.
The Thornton incipit calls it the Myrrour of Seynt Edmonde; modern scholarship also uses Speculum Religiosorum and Speculum Ecclesiae for related source states.
The Thornton headings also use Religio Sancti Spiritus and Religio Munda.

The works share pastoral and devotional concerns but have separate authorship, translation histories, dates, and witness traditions.
